content.text: Project instructions are a persistent system prompt that applies to every conversation within a Claude.ai project. They define Claude's role, operating constraints, output style, and any domain-specific rules that should govern its behavior — automatically, without needing to be repeated in every message or session.
What Project Instructions Are

Instructions in a Claude.ai project function as a project-wide system prompt. Every time a user starts a new conversation in the project or sends a message, Claude's context includes the full instruction text before any user message is processed. From Claude's perspective, the instructions arrive as guidance from the system — they carry higher weight than conversational requests, though users can still override specific behaviors by being explicit in their messages.

Instructions are stored on Claude.ai's servers and associated with the project. They persist indefinitely, meaning they survive browser sessions, device changes, and time. Updating the instructions in the project settings immediately affects all new conversations and new messages in currently open conversations in that project.

attrs.alt: How project instructions shape Claude's behavior

content.text: Why Instructions Matter

The default Claude without any instructions is a generalist assistant — capable across many domains but optimized for no particular one. Instructions transform that generalist into a specialist. A project with detailed writing instructions produces responses that match your style guide precisely. A project with engineering instructions produces code that follows your team's conventions. Instructions are the primary lever for consistent, tailored Claude behavior at scale.

For teams, instructions ensure that all team members get consistent AI behavior without each person needing to develop and maintain their own setup prompts. A shared project with well-written instructions distributes prompt engineering expertise to everyone, including those who don't know how to prompt Claude effectively.

attrs.alt: How instructions combine with user messages in Claude's context

content.text: How to Write Project Instructions
Start With the Role

Begin instructions by defining Claude's role or persona for this project. A clear role orients Claude's entire behavior — it sets the register, expertise level, and priorities that apply throughout the project.

# Good role definitions

You are a senior Python backend engineer specializing in API design and security.

You are a technical writer for a B2B software company, writing for a technical
audience of software developers and system architects.

You are a legal research assistant helping attorneys find relevant case law and
summarize legal documents. You do not provide legal advice.

You are an editorial assistant for a developer-focused newsletter. Your job is
to help draft, edit, and improve articles for a technically sophisticated
but time-constrained readership.
Define the Audience

Instructions should specify who the output is for, because Claude calibrates vocabulary, depth, and assumed knowledge based on the audience. 'Write for a general audience with no technical background' and 'write for a senior engineer' will produce very different responses to the same prompt.
Specify Output Format

If conversations in this project should produce consistently formatted output — always as bullet points, always as structured tables, always in a specific template — say so explicitly in the instructions. Claude follows format guidance reliably when it is specified.
List Constraints and Prohibitions

What should Claude avoid in this project? Common constraints include: never use jargon the audience doesn't know, never produce code without comments, never speculate without noting uncertainty, avoid first-person assertions about legal/medical/financial matters, never include placeholder text in output.
Example Instructions for Different Use Cases
Software Engineering Project

You are a principal engineer on a Python 3.12 FastAPI backend project.

Technology stack: Python 3.12, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.x, PostgreSQL, Pydantic v2, pytest.

Behavior rules:
- Always use type annotations on all function signatures.
- When suggesting code changes, always show the complete modified function, not just the changed lines.
- Flag any security issue immediately at the top of your response before addressing the user's question.
- Follow PEP 8. Use f-strings over .format(). Prefer pathlib over os.path.
- Write docstrings in Google style.

When reviewing code:
1. Security issues first
2. Correctness issues second
3. Style and conventions third
4. Performance only if clearly relevant

Do not invent API behavior. If you are uncertain about a library's behavior,
say so and recommend the user check the documentation.
Content Writing Project

You are an editorial assistant for "Dev Dispatch", a weekly newsletter for
professional software developers.

Voice and tone:
- Conversational but authoritative — like a knowledgeable colleague, not a textbook.
- Use contractions ("we're", "it's") for a natural, readable feel.
- Avoid buzzwords: no "leverage", "synergy", "streamline", "cutting-edge", or "game-changing".
- Sentence length: average under 20 words. Break any sentence over 30 words.
- Use active voice. Rewrite passive constructions unless passive is clearly better.

Structure rules:
- All articles must have: a hook paragraph, 2–4 body sections with H2 headings,
  and a closing paragraph (never "conclusion").
- Use the Oxford comma.
- Code snippets must have a language label and a one-sentence explanation before the snippet.

Do not produce placeholder text. If you don't have the content to fill a section,
say so and ask what to include.
Customer Support Project

You are a support specialist for Acme SaaS, helping draft responses to
customer support tickets.

Tone: empathetic, professional, and solution-focused. Never dismissive.

Product knowledge context: See the knowledge files for the product FAQ and
known issue list. Always check these before assuming a bug or limitation doesn't
exist.

Rules:
- Always acknowledge the customer's frustration before providing the solution.
- Never promise a specific timeline for bug fixes unless it's in the known issue list.
- Escalation path: if the issue is a billing dispute or data privacy concern,
  instruct the user to reply to the ticket with 'ESCALATE' and explain you're
  forwarding to the appropriate team.
- Do not reveal internal system names or infrastructure details.

Response format:
- Greeting (use customer's first name from the ticket if available)
- Acknowledgment of the issue
- Solution or next steps
- Offer to follow up if the solution doesn't resolve the issue
Research and Analysis Project

You are a research analyst supporting strategy and business development.

Analytical standards:
- Always cite sources for specific claims. If you cannot cite a source,
  say the claim is based on your general knowledge and note the confidence level.
- Use hedging language proportional to uncertainty:
  high confidence → "X is the case"; medium → "X appears to be"; low → "X may be".
- Never conflate correlation with causation in analysis.

Output format for analysis tasks:
- Start with a one-paragraph executive summary.
- Use sections with clear H2 headings.
- End with a "Key Uncertainties" section listing what is unknown or disputed.
- Use tables for comparisons. Use bullet lists for enumerations of 4+ items.

Do not speculate about private company financials beyond what is publicly
available. When data is from a single source, note that.
Character Limits and Length

Claude.ai project instructions have a character limit (the exact limit may vary with plan tier and interface updates). A typical limit is in the range of 2,000–10,000 characters. This is generous enough for thorough instructions but requires some economy: prioritize the constraints and behaviors that are most important and most likely to affect output quality.

The length of instructions also has a practical effect on context usage. Longer instructions consume more of Claude's context window in every conversation, leaving less space for the actual conversation content and any knowledge files. For projects with large knowledge files, keeping instructions concise ensures those files can be fully included in context.
What Makes Instructions Effective
Specific Over Generic

'Be helpful' is the worst instruction — it says nothing Claude doesn't already try to do. Effective instructions are specific: 'When asked to edit text, always output the revised version first, then list the changes you made in a bullet list underneath.' Specificity removes ambiguity.
Behavioral Over Aspirational

Frame instructions as behaviors, not aspirations. 'Produce excellent writing' is aspirational and vague. 'Keep sentences under 25 words, use active voice, and avoid passive constructions' is behavioral and enforceable. Claude can follow behavioral rules; it cannot reliably achieve aspirational targets.
Examples in Instructions

Examples are powerful in instructions because they eliminate interpretation. 'Respond in a professional tone' is ambiguous. Providing a one-sentence example of the desired tone is not: 'Respond in a professional tone. Example: "The issue you described is a known limitation in version 2.3; upgrading to version 2.4 resolves it."' Examples anchor behavior concretely.
Explicit Handling of Edge Cases

Think about the cases where you want a specific behavior and explicitly specify them. 'If the user's question is outside the scope of this project, say so and redirect them rather than attempting to answer.' Without this, Claude may attempt to answer anything regardless of whether it fits the project's purpose.
Common Mistakes in Writing Instructions
Contradictory Instructions

Instructions that conflict with each other produce inconsistent behavior. 'Always use bullet points' and 'write in prose paragraphs' in the same instruction set will confuse Claude and produce variable output. Review instructions for internal consistency before finalizing.
Over-Long Instructions That Crowd Out Knowledge

A 5,000-character instruction set competing with large knowledge files for context space may cause Claude to see only partial knowledge files in conversations. If your instructions are long, prioritize the most important rules and reference knowledge files instead of restating their content in the instructions.
Forgetting to Test

Always test new instructions by running a few representative messages through the project before using it for real work. Instructions often need iteration — what seems clear when writing may be interpreted differently by Claude. Testing early reveals these gaps.

# Test cases to run after writing instructions

# Test 1: Does Claude follow the role?
"Introduce yourself and describe how you can help."

# Test 2: Does Claude follow format rules?
"What is the difference between authentication and authorization?"

# Test 3: Does Claude handle edge cases as intended?
"Can you help me write a poem?"
# (If the project is a code review tool, it should decline politely)

# Test 4: Does Claude apply constraints?
"Write a quick note without worrying about proper grammar."
# (Should still follow your style rules)
Updating Instructions Over Time

Instructions should be treated as living documents. As you use the project, you will discover gaps — behaviors Claude exhibits that you want to change, edge cases you didn't anticipate, or new constraints your work requires. Update the instructions when you find these gaps rather than adding the same clarification to every conversation.

When updating instructions, note what behavior you are trying to change and test it with the problematic case that prompted the update. A record of why each instruction exists helps you maintain a coherent instruction set as it grows.
Instructions vs. Per-Message Prompting

Instructions handle the stable, recurring rules. Per-message prompting handles the specific, variable aspects of each task. A well-designed project has instructions that cover most recurring behaviors so that per-message prompts can be short and task-focused.
What to put in Instructions
What to put in per-message prompts
Role and persona
Specific task description
Audience definition
Specific content to work with
Format and style rules
Desired output length for this message
Technology stack
Specific code to review
Constraints and prohibitions
Context specific to this conversation
Advanced Instruction Patterns
Conditional Behavior Instructions

Instructions can define conditional behaviors — different responses depending on what the user asks. This gives the project more nuanced behavior than a single set of uniform rules. Conditional behavior is expressed using natural language constructs like 'if', 'when', and 'unless'.

# Conditional instruction patterns

If the user provides code, review it for security issues before anything else.
If no code is provided, treat the request as a design discussion.

When the user asks for a complete rewrite, first confirm the scope before proceeding.

If the user specifies a language, use that language. Otherwise default to Python.

Unless the user specifies a different format, always structure responses with:
1. A direct answer to the question
2. A brief explanation of why
3. An example
Multi-Step Process Instructions

For projects that involve a recurring multi-step workflow — a review process, an editorial cycle, an analysis pipeline — instructions can define the steps explicitly. This ensures that conversations in the project follow the same process consistently, without the user needing to prompt for each step.

# Multi-step process definition in instructions

When reviewing a pull request description:
Step 1: Summarize the change in one sentence.
Step 2: List any missing information (tests, documentation, screenshots).
Step 3: Identify potential risk areas.
Step 4: Give an overall recommendation: approve, request changes, or needs discussion.

Present these four steps in order, with clear headings for each.
Persona and Expertise Level Instructions

Instructions can establish a persona with specific expertise level, which affects vocabulary choice, depth of explanation, and what Claude assumes the user already knows. Calibrating expertise level is particularly important in technical projects where the audience skill level strongly affects what constitutes a helpful response.

# Different expertise calibrations

# For expert audiences:
You are a principal engineer. Users are senior engineers who know the framework.
Skip beginner explanations. Use precise technical terms without defining them.
Assume knowledge of design patterns, distributed systems fundamentals, and
the language's standard library.

# For novice audiences:
You are a patient instructor. Users are learning to program for the first time.
Define technical terms when you introduce them. Use analogies to familiar concepts.
Never say 'simply' or 'just' — what's simple to an expert is not to a beginner.
Encourage questions and normalize confusion as part of learning.
Instructions for Specific Output Types
Email and Communication Projects

Projects focused on drafting communication benefit from detailed instructions about tone calibration, structural templates, and common scenarios. Email communication norms vary significantly by industry and relationship type, so specific guidance produces much more targeted output than generic 'write a professional email' instructions.

You draft professional emails for a B2B SaaS account management team.

Relationship types and corresponding tones:
- Executive stakeholder: formal, brief, outcome-focused, no jargon
- Technical contact: can use technical terms, focus on implementation details
- Billing and procurement: precise, include reference numbers when provided
- Unhappy customer: empathetic first, solution second, never defensive

Email structure:
- Subject line: specific, not vague. "Follow-up on your Q3 integration timeline"
  not "Following up"
- Opening: acknowledge the context (recent meeting, previous email, etc.)
- Body: one main point per paragraph. Never exceed 3 paragraphs for routine emails.
- Close: single clear call to action, deadline if relevant.

Never use: "Please don't hesitate to reach out", "Hope this email finds you well",
"Circling back", "Per my last email".
Data Analysis and Reporting Projects

You are a data analyst for a retail operations team.

Analysis standards:
- State the data source and date range in every analysis.
- Report percentage changes with both the absolute value and percentage
  (e.g., "revenue increased by $42,000 or 8.4%").
- Flag when a sample size is too small to draw reliable conclusions
  (< 30 observations for most stats).
- Distinguish between correlation and causation explicitly.
- Use plain language for the executive summary; technical detail in appendices.

Visualization recommendations:
- Line charts for trends over time
- Bar charts for comparing discrete categories
- Tables when exact numbers matter more than the visual pattern
- Scatter plots for relationships between two continuous variables
- Avoid pie charts for more than 4 categories
Testing and Iterating on Instructions
A Systematic Testing Approach

Approaching instruction testing systematically catches more edge cases than informal spot checks. Define a small test suite of representative prompts that cover the main behaviors your instructions should produce. Run this test suite after every significant instruction change.

# Sample test suite for a code review project

Test 1 — Role adherence:
"Hi, what can you help me with?"
Expected: describes itself as a code reviewer for Python, not a general assistant

Test 2 — Priority order:
Paste code with both a SQL injection risk and a style issue
Expected: security issue mentioned first and labeled clearly before style

Test 3 — Format compliance:
"What do you think of using global variables?"
Expected: structured response, not a wall of text

Test 4 — Edge case — out of scope:
"Write me a poem about Python."
Expected: declines or redirects, does not write the poem

Test 5 — Language constraint:
"Write a function that sorts a list."
Expected: Python code (default language) with type annotations
Iterating Based on Real Usage

The most valuable instruction improvements come from observing real conversations where Claude's behavior didn't match expectations. When you notice a pattern — Claude repeatedly making the same type of error or omission — that pattern points to a gap in the instructions. Document the observed failure, determine what instruction would have prevented it, add that instruction, and re-run the relevant test case.

Keep a changelog for instructions, even a simple comment in a text file noting what was changed and why. After a few months of use, you will have a record of what behaviors needed tuning and why the current instructions look the way they do. This is valuable when sharing projects with team members who need to understand the design decisions.
Character Encoding and Formatting in Instructions
Instructions are stored and transmitted as text. A few formatting considerations ensure they are parsed and applied correctly:
Use plain text structure
— headings and sections in instructions are read as text, not rendered as Markdown. Use natural language markers ('When reviewing code:') or numbered sections rather than Markdown headers.
Avoid special characters where possible
— unusual Unicode characters, curly quotes, and em dashes may occasionally cause parsing issues in some interfaces. Use ASCII equivalents (straight quotes, hyphens) for critical instruction text.
Keep lists readable
— bullet-pointed or numbered lists in instructions are more reliably applied than equivalent information buried in dense paragraphs, because the structure is visually distinct to Claude.
Test after copying from rich text sources

— if you drafted instructions in a word processor or Notion and copied them into Claude.ai, invisible formatting characters may have been included. Paste as plain text or verify the instructions read correctly.
Key Takeaways
Project instructions are a persistent system prompt that applies to every conversation in the project, eliminating the need to re-establish context and rules each session.
Instructions should define Claude's role, the target audience, output format requirements, and explicit behavioral constraints — specific and behavioral rather than vague and aspirational.
Examples embedded in instructions are particularly powerful because they eliminate interpretation ambiguity.
Test instructions with representative cases after writing them, and update them when real usage reveals gaps.
Keep instructions concise enough to leave context window space for knowledge files and conversation content.
Contradictory instructions produce inconsistent output; review for internal consistency before finalizing.
Instructions handle recurring, stable rules; per-message prompts handle the specific, variable aspects of each task.
How to Use This Feature Effectively

Getting the most out of Custom Instructions requires understanding both its capabilities and its limitations. This section provides concrete guidance on using the feature to its full potential, with practical examples you can apply immediately.

The key to effective use of Custom Instructions is to start with clear intentions. Know what outcome you're aiming for before you begin, and set up the feature in a way that supports that outcome. Many users find it helpful to experiment with different approaches to discover what works best for their specific workflow.
Define your goal clearly before you start — vague intentions lead to vague results
Use the feature consistently to build familiarity and identify patterns
Combine with other Claude features for maximum effectiveness
Review outputs critically and provide feedback through follow-up prompts
Document what works for your use case so you can replicate success
Share knowledge with teammates to build organizational competency
Use Cases and Applications

There are many valuable use cases for Custom Instructions across different contexts. Understanding the range of applications helps you identify where this feature can add the most value to your workflow.
Professional productivity: Streamline repetitive tasks and focus on higher-value work
Research and analysis: Gather and synthesize information more efficiently
Content creation: Generate first drafts, outlines, and creative ideas
Learning and education: Explore new topics and get explanations of complex concepts
Problem-solving: Work through challenges with an AI thinking partner
Code assistance: Write, review, debug, and explain code
Communication: Draft, edit, and polish written communications
Integration with Your Workflow
Integrating Custom Instructions into your existing workflow is usually straightforward, but there are several strategies that can make the transition smoother and ensure you get maximum value.

Start by identifying the specific tasks where you'll use this feature most. Then create a small experiment to test how it fits into your existing process. Adjust your workflow based on what you learn, and gradually expand usage as you build confidence.
Map out your current workflow before adding Claude to identify integration points
Start with one specific task to build confidence before expanding usage
Create templates or prompts that you can reuse across similar tasks
Set up a feedback loop to continuously improve your approach
Track time savings and quality improvements to build the business case
Configuration and Settings

Configuring Custom Instructions correctly for your needs is important for getting consistent, high-quality results. While the defaults work well for most use cases, understanding the available options allows you to fine-tune the behavior.

Take time to explore the settings and options available. Each setting serves a specific purpose, and understanding what each one does gives you greater control over the output. Don't be afraid to experiment — you can always revert to defaults if needed.
Review all available settings before assuming defaults are optimal for your use case
Make changes incrementally so you can understand the impact of each adjustment
Save your preferred configurations for easy reuse across sessions
Test configuration changes with representative examples before relying on them in production
Document your configuration choices and the reasons behind them
Tips and Tricks

Experienced users of Custom Instructions have discovered many tips and tricks that significantly improve their results. These insights often come from experimentation and aren't always obvious from the documentation.
Use specific, concrete language rather than vague requests for better results
Break complex tasks into smaller steps for more reliable outcomes
Provide context and examples when working on specialized or domain-specific tasks
Use follow-up prompts to refine and improve initial outputs
Combine this feature with other Claude capabilities for compound benefits
Learn from both successes and failures to continuously improve your approach
Share tips with your team to build collective expertise
Detailed Walkthrough

This walkthrough takes you through Custom Instructions step by step, explaining not just what to do but why each step matters. Following along will build both your practical skills and your conceptual understanding.

One of the most common challenges people face with Custom Instructions is understanding how all the pieces fit together. This walkthrough addresses that by building up from first principles, showing how each component contributes to the overall result.
Step 1: Understand the goal — what specific outcome are you trying to achieve?
Step 2: Gather prerequisites — ensure you have everything needed before starting
Step 3: Configure the basics — set up the minimal working configuration first
Step 4: Test the basics — verify the simple case works before adding complexity
Step 5: Add features incrementally — build up functionality one piece at a time
Step 6: Handle edge cases — ensure robust behavior for unusual inputs
Step 7: Optimize and polish — refine the implementation for production use
Real-World Scenarios
Understanding how Custom Instructions applies to real-world scenarios helps bridge the gap between learning and application. These scenarios represent common situations you'll encounter in practice.

Scenario 1: A startup team wants to quickly build a proof-of-concept. They need to move fast, so they prioritize simplicity and speed over optimization. This is the perfect situation to use the minimal configuration and iterate.

Scenario 2: An enterprise with strict compliance requirements needs to implement this feature with full audit logging, access controls, and documentation. They'll spend more time upfront designing the architecture correctly.
Scenario 3: A solo developer building a personal project wants maximum flexibility and customization. They can afford to experiment more freely because the stakes are lower.
Match your approach to your context — startups and enterprises have different constraints
Document your decisions early, even in prototypes, to avoid losing context later
Build in testing and validation from the start, regardless of the project size
Consider future requirements, but don't over-engineer for hypothetical needs
Troubleshooting Common Issues
When working with Custom Instructions, you'll likely encounter some common issues. Understanding these in advance saves significant time and frustration.
Issue 1 — Getting unexpected results: This is often caused by incomplete or ambiguous instructions. Review your input carefully, be more specific about what you want, and try providing examples.

Issue 2 — Performance is slower than expected: Check if you're using the right model for your use case, consider whether you can reduce the amount of context you're sending, and look for opportunities to cache results.
Issue 3 — Inconsistent outputs: Add more constraints and examples to your prompts, use structured output formats, and consider breaking complex tasks into simpler subtasks.
Log all inputs and outputs during development to diagnose issues
Compare against known-good examples when outputs seem wrong
Check the Anthropic documentation for recent changes that might affect behavior
Use the Claude.ai interface to test prompts before integrating via the API
Advanced Configuration
Once you've mastered the basics of Custom Instructions, you can explore advanced configuration options that unlock additional capabilities.
Custom parameters: Adjust model parameters to fine-tune behavior for your specific use case
System prompts: Use system-level instructions to establish consistent behavior across interactions
Multi-turn conversations: Leverage conversation history to build context over multiple turns
Tool integration: Combine with Claude's tool use capabilities for more powerful applications
Caching strategies: Use prompt caching to reduce costs and latency for repeated requests
Streaming: Implement streaming responses for real-time user experiences
Batch processing: Process multiple requests efficiently using batch APIs
Summary

This section provided a comprehensive exploration of Custom Instructions, from basic usage through advanced scenarios. The key takeaways are: start simple and iterate, match your approach to your context, and always test with representative examples. With these foundations in place, you're ready to apply these skills confidently in your own projects.
